title: DARPA and U.S. Air Force fly AI-controlled F-16
summary: DARPA and the U.S. Air Force have successfully flown an AI-controlled F-16, allowing a pilot to toggle between human and AI control during flight as part of the Air Combat Evolution \(ACE\) program. This milestone demonstrates progress in autonomous air combat and human-machine teaming, potentially leading to AI wingmen that can operate alongside human pilots in future missions. The aircraft uses a novel interface that allows the pilot to toggle control with a flip of a switch, ensuring a safe environment for human-on-the-loop experimentation. However, critics note that sudden handover from AI to human can be challenging in high-stress situations.
context: The flight is part of DARPA&\#x27;s Air Combat Evolution \(ACE\) program, which aims to increase trust in autonomous combat technology through human-machine collaborative dogfighting. The aircraft used is the X-62A VISTA, a modified F-16D that serves as a variable stability test bed. Previous ACE milestones include the AlphaDogfight Trials, where AI defeated human pilots in simulated dogfights.
discussion: Comments range from ironic Terminator references to technical critiques. User SoftTalker questions the safety of sudden handover from AI to human pilot, citing known automation issues. Others argue that carrying a pilot on an AI-controlled fighter defeats the purpose, comparing it to a drone with unnecessary life support.
